overview.body: All semester, you've been building Deep in the Heart: A Lone Star Journey with dictionaries, functions, and files. It works — but the code is scattered. City data lives in one place, distance lookups in another, and game logic somewhere else. This week, everything changes. You'll create a City class that bundles data and behavior together. A City object will know its own name, connections, and how to display itself. Welcome to Object-Oriented Programming.

mentorQuote.quote: Testing classes is straightforward: create an object, call every method, check every attribute. If city.name returns the right name and city.is_dead_end() correctly identifies dead ends, your class works. Build the test before you build the class!

objectives: An __init__ constructor that initializes private attributes
Getter methods to safely access private data
Connection methods to add routes and check distances
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 10: Classes and Object-Oriented Programming
programRequirements: City MUST have getter methods for name, type, and tagline
lab-5-1.py MUST import and test the City class
overview.filenameNote: Submit city.py (the City class definition) and lab-5-1.py (the test program that imports and uses City) this week.
overview.additionalFilesNote: CodeGrade tests in isolation, both files must be present.

timeGuide.strategy: OOP is a new way of thinking. If it feels weird at first, that's normal! Build one method at a time, test it, then move on. By the end of this lab, the pattern will start clicking.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's the structure of what you're building. city.py contains nothing but the class definition — no game code, no main(). lab-5-1.py imports it and tests every piece. Each checkpoint adds one section to the class.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- city.py ----
class City:

    # --- Constructor ---
    __init__(self, name, city_type, tagline)   stores 4 private attributes
        self.__name          = name
        self.__city_type     = city_type
        self.__tagline       = tagline
        self.__connections   = {}              empty dict to start

    # --- Getter Methods ---   [CP2]
    get_name(self)                returns self.__name
    get_type(self)                returns self.__city_type
    get_tagline(self)             returns self.__tagline

    # --- Connection Methods ---   [CP3]
    add_connection(self, city_name, distance)   stores in self.__connections
    get_distance_to(self, city_name)            returns distance or -1
    get_destination_names(self)                 returns list of connected names

    # --- Status Methods ---   [CP4]
    is_win_city(self)             returns True if type == 'WIN'
    is_dead_end(self)             returns True if type == 'DEAD_END'

    # --- Display Methods ---   [CP4]
    display_destinations(self)    prints numbered menu of connections
    __str__(self)                 returns 'San Antonio (START)'


# ---- lab-5-1.py ----   [CP5]
from city import City

main():
    Create 4 City objects (San Antonio, Houston, Corpus Christi, Laredo)
    Add connections between them
    Test all getter methods
    Test __str__ by printing each city
    Test connection methods
    Test is_win_city() and is_dead_end()
    Call display_destinations()

if __name__ == '__main__': -> main()

checkpoints.title: File Setup & Constructor
checkpoints.subtitle: Create city.py and define the City class with its __init__ constructor.

content.body: The foundation of your City class: a properly documented file, the class definition, and an __init__ method that initializes four private attributes.

This is the moment everything changes. Instead of juggling separate dictionaries for city names, distances, and info, a City object owns its own data. Every game, app, and system you'll build professionally uses objects to bundle related data and behavior together. Master this pattern and you're thinking like a software engineer.

content.role: Director of Technology, Bat City Collective
content.quote: In Module 4, you had CITY_INFO dictionaries and CITY_DISTANCES dictionaries and functions scattered everywhere. Now? A City object knows its own data. The constructor is the birth certificate — it says exactly what a City needs to exist.

references.description: You typed a CellPhone class with __init__ that accepted parameters and stored them in private attributes like self.__manufact. Your City class follows the same pattern — accept name, city_type, and tagline, then store them with double-underscore prefixes.

items: Add the module docstring at the top:

content.code:
"""
city.py
Name: Your Name
abc123: your_abc123
Section: 001
Description: City class for the Deep in the Heart road trip game.

Chapter 10 Concepts:
- Class definition with class keyword
- __init__ constructor
- Private attributes with __ prefix
- Getter methods
- __str__ method
"""

items: Define the City class and add a class docstring:

content.code:
class City:
    """
    Represents a city in the Texas road trip game.

    Attributes (private):
        __name (str): City name
        __city_type (str): Type of city
        __tagline (str): City motto
        __connections (dict): Connected cities and distances
    """

items: Write the __init__ method that accepts name, city_type, and tagline parameters
Inside __init__, store each parameter as a private attribute: self.__name = name, self.__city_type = city_type, self.__tagline = tagline
Also initialize an empty dictionary for connections: self.__connections = {}

content.body: Step 1: Add this test code at the bottom of city.py (temporarily):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
san_antonio = City('San Antonio', 'START', 'Home of the Alamo')
print('City object created successfully!')
print(type(san_antonio))

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Forgetting self as first parameter
Every method needs self first — it's how Python knows which object to work with
Using name instead of self.__name
Attributes need the self. prefix to belong to the object
Naming the method _init_ (single underscores)
Must be __init__ with DOUBLE underscores on both sides

checkpoints.title: Getter Methods
checkpoints.subtitle: Add methods that return the values of private attributes.

content.body: Three getter methods that provide controlled access to your City's private data. Outside code can read the city's name but can't accidentally change it.

references.description: Remember get_manufact(), get_model(), and get_retail_price()? Each just returned self.__attribute. Your getters work the same way.

items: Add a comment # --- Getter Methods --- below __init__
Add a docstring to each method (e.g., """Return the city's name.""")

content.body: Step 1: Add this test code at the bottom of city.py:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
san_antonio = City('San Antonio', 'START', 'Home of the Alamo')
print('Testing getter methods:')
print(f'  Name: {san_antonio.get_name()}')
print(f'  Type: {san_antonio.get_type()}')
print(f'  Tagline: {san_antonio.get_tagline()}')

content.output:
Testing getter methods:
  Name: San Antonio
  Type: START
  Tagline: Home of the Alamo

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Accessing self.name instead of self.__name
The attribute is private (double underscore) — must match exactly
Using print() instead of return
Getters should return the value, not print it — the caller decides what to do with it
Forgetting parentheses when calling: city.get_name
Without () you get the method object, not its return value

checkpoints.title: Connection Methods
checkpoints.subtitle: Add methods to manage city connections — adding routes and checking distances.

content.body: Three methods that let cities know about their road connections. This replaces the old CITY_DISTANCES dictionary — now each city manages its own connections!

items: Define add_connection(self, city_name, distance) that stores the connection:
Define get_distance_to(self, city_name) that returns the distance, or -1 if not connected:
Define get_destination_names(self) that returns a list of connected city names:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 4
san_antonio = City('San Antonio', 'START', 'Home of the Alamo')
san_antonio.add_connection('Houston', 200)
san_antonio.add_connection('Corpus Christi', 150)
san_antonio.add_connection('Laredo', 160)

print('Testing connection methods:')
print(f'  Distance to Houston: {san_antonio.get_distance_to("Houston")} miles')
print(f'  Distance to Dallas: {san_antonio.get_distance_to("Dallas")} miles')
print(f'  Destinations: {san_antonio.get_destination_names()}')

content.output:
Testing connection methods:
  Distance to Houston: 200 miles
  Distance to Dallas: -1 miles
  Destinations: ['Houston', 'Corpus Christi', 'Laredo']

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Using self.__connections[city_name] for get_distance_to
KeyError if city not connected — use .get(city_name, -1) for safe access
Returning self.__connections.keys() directly
Returns a dict_keys object, not a list — wrap with list()
Forgetting to add docstrings to methods
CodeGrade checks for documentation — every method needs a docstring

checkpoints.title: Status Methods & __str__
checkpoints.subtitle: Add methods to check city type and a __str__ method for nice printing.

content.body: Methods that let you ask a city about itself: "Are you a win city?" "Are you a dead end?" Plus a display method and the special __str__ method that Python calls when you print() a City object.

references.description: You typed a __str__ method that returned a formatted string. Python automatically calls this when you print() an object. Your City's __str__ should return something like 'San Antonio (START)'.

items: Define is_win_city(self) that returns True if self.__city_type == 'WIN' — add a docstring
Define is_dead_end(self) that returns True if self.__city_type == 'DEAD_END' — add a docstring
Define display_destinations(self) that prints a numbered menu of connections:

content.code:
def display_destinations(self):
    """Display available destinations from this city."""
    print(f'From {self.__name}, you can travel to:')
    option_num = 1
    for dest_name, distance in self.__connections.items():
        print(f'  {option_num}. {dest_name} - {distance} miles')
        option_num = option_num + 1

items: Define __str__(self) that returns a string like 'San Antonio (START)':

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 5
corpus = City('Corpus Christi', 'WIN', 'Sparkling City by the Sea')
laredo = City('Laredo', 'DEAD_END', 'Gateway to Mexico')
san_antonio = City('San Antonio', 'START', 'Home of the Alamo')

san_antonio.add_connection('Houston', 200)
san_antonio.add_connection('Corpus Christi', 150)

print('Testing __str__:')
print(f'  {san_antonio}')
print(f'  {corpus}')
print(f'  {laredo}')
print()
print('Testing status methods:')
print(f'  Corpus is_win_city: {corpus.is_win_city()}')
print(f'  Laredo is_dead_end: {laredo.is_dead_end()}')
print()
print('Testing display_destinations:')
san_antonio.display_destinations()

content.output:
Testing __str__:
  San Antonio (START)
  Corpus Christi (WIN)
  Laredo (DEAD_END)

Testing status methods:
  Corpus is_win_city: True
  Laredo is_dead_end: True

Testing display_destinations:
From San Antonio, you can travel to:
  1. Houston - 200 miles
  2. Corpus Christi - 150 miles

content.body: Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Using print() inside __str__
__str__ must return a string — Python calls it when you print the object
Comparing type with 'win' (lowercase)
City types are uppercase ('WIN', 'DEAD_END') — case must match exactly
Naming method _str_ (single underscores)
Must be __str__ with DOUBLE underscores — that's how Python recognizes it

items: display_destinations() method printing numbered menu, with docstring
__str__() method returning formatted string, with docstring

checkpoints.title: Complete Test Program
checkpoints.subtitle: Create lab-5-1.py that imports City and runs a comprehensive test.

content.body: A separate test program that imports your City class and demonstrates all its capabilities. This is the file you'll submit to CodeGrade along with city.py.

references.description: You typed import cellphone then created objects with cellphone.CellPhone(...). Your test program uses from city import City so you can write City(...) directly.

content.quote: Think about what you've built: city.py contains the City class, and lab-5-1.py tests it. Two separate files, two separate concerns. When we add the full game in Lab 5.2, we import the same City class. That's code reuse — you write it once, use it everywhere. This is how professional projects stay organized!

items: Add the standard header docstring with your name, abc123, section, and description
Import the City class: from city import City

Define a main() function that creates 4 City objects (San Antonio, Houston, Corpus Christi, Laredo), adds connections between them, and tests all methods — getters, __str__, connection methods, status methods, and display_destinations()

content.output:
==================================================
LAB 5.1: MEET THE CITIES
Testing the City Class
==================================================

Creating cities...

Testing getter methods:
  Name: San Antonio
  Type: START
  Tagline: Home of the Alamo

Testing __str__ method (just print the object):
  San Antonio (START)
  Houston (HUB)
  Corpus Christi (WIN)
  Laredo (DEAD_END)

Adding connections...
Done!

Testing connection methods:
  San Antonio to Houston: 200 miles
  San Antonio to Dallas: -1 miles

Destinations from San Antonio:
  - Houston
  - Corpus Christi
  - Laredo

Testing display_destinations():
From San Antonio, you can travel to:
  1. Houston - 200 miles
  2. Corpus Christi - 150 miles
  3. Laredo - 160 miles

Testing is_win_city() and is_dead_end():
  Corpus Christi is_win_city: True
  Houston is_win_city: False
  Laredo is_dead_end: True
  Houston is_dead_end: False

==================================================
SUCCESS! City class works correctly.
==================================================

Key OOP concepts demonstrated:
  - Created a class with private attributes
  - Used __init__ to initialize objects
  - Created getter methods for data access
  - Used __str__ for printing objects

rows: Importing city.py instead of from city import City
Need to import the class itself, not the filename
Missing if __name__ == '__main__':
Test code runs when imported by other files — the guard prevents this
Files not in the same directory
city.py and lab-5-1.py must be in the same folder

items: main() function that tests all City methods

beforeYouSubmit: lab-5-1.py has header docstring with your name, abc123, and section
lab-5-1.py imports City with from city import City
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 10 Sections 10.1–10.3 ONLY. Classes, constructors, private attributes, getter methods, and special methods only. Inheritance and multiple classes come in future labs.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–10 (Sections 10.1–10.3 introduced this module; all prior techniques fully permitted)

warnings: Submit BOTH files: CodeGrade will test that lab-5-1.py can import your City class from city.py. If you only submit one file, the import will fail.
Make sure city.py has no test code at the bottom — it should only contain the class definition.

needHelp.chapterResource: Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example; your primary reference for this lab
ifYouGetStuck: Re-read the BookEx exercises — CellPhone and BankAccount are your templates
Check your indentation — methods must be indented inside the class
Remember self — every method's first parameter is self
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
Come to Zoom or office hours — OOP is tricky at first; we can help!
needHelp.discordNote: Post in the course Discord with your checkpoint number, what you expected to happen, and what actually happened. Include the specific error message if you have one.
